cluster_name: Ukraine-Russia drone war and Crimea fuel crisis
event_summary: Ukraine escalated drone strikes on Russia's Crimea supply corridor and deep-interior refineries in Tatarstan and Samara, triggering the worst fuel crisis in Crimea since 2014 and suspending rail service to the peninsula. Russia simultaneously launched drone barrages on Kyiv, Mykolaiv, Zaporizhzhia, Kharkiv, and Sumy rail infrastructure, killing civilians on both sides.
tldr: Headline framing is dominated by the mutual drone-war narrative, but the Crimea fuel and supply-chain crisis emerges as the distinctive cross-regional sub-story: wire, UK, and RFE/RL outlets all advertise it at homepage level while most others stay in the generic strike-exchange register. Ukrainian outlets uniquely surface granular casualty counts and settlement-attack tallies; Russian-aligned outlets lead on drone-intercept numbers and civilian disruption from Ukraine's strikes.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to frame Ukraine's military successes as 'Putin's humiliation' — uniquely personalising the campaign around Putin's political defeat rather than operational or tactical terms used by all other outlets. Produces the most sensationalised headline in the corpus.

Only outlet to lead with the technology-advantage angle ('Ukraine's AI drones have given Kyiv a fresh edge on the battlefield'), the sole headline foregrounding capability shift rather than the daily strike-and-casualties cycle dominant across all other buckets.

The sole Kremlin-controlled state wire in the corpus, leading entirely on defensive success ('Thirteen drones shot down on approach to Moscow today') — the sharpest headline inversion relative to Ukrainian and Western outlets, which frame the same period as Ukrainian offensive escalation.

Only outlet to explicitly link Ukrainian drone timing to Russia's premier annual investment forum (St. Petersburg SPIEF), framing the strikes as deliberate geopolitical messaging against Russian economic signaling rather than routine military operations.

bottom_line: At the headline layer, the Ukraine-Russia story resolves into two advertised narratives: a ubiquitous mutual drone-war exchange frame (present across all 13 buckets) and a Crimea supply-chain collapse frame (wire, UK, RFE/RL) — with Daily Mail uniquely pitching a Putin-humiliation angle and TASS alone inverting the story into a Russian air-defence success.
